title: New Start Act of 2025
plain_summary: This bill creates a program called New Start that gives money to groups that teach people in prison or people who used to be in prison how to start their own businesses. The program will run for five years and is meant to help these people become business owners instead of having trouble finding regular jobs. The goal is to help them stay out of trouble and not commit crimes again by giving them a way to make money legally.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship.

sections.title: Section 1 Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the New Start Act of two thousand twenty five.

sections.title: Section 2 Findings
sections.text: Congress has learned these important facts. Every year about six hundred thousand people are released from prison and go back to their communities. Almost seventy seven out of every one hundred of these people will commit crimes again within five years. About forty eight and a half out of every one hundred formerly imprisoned people will stay unemployed or earn very little money for one year after getting out of prison. This makes them more likely to commit crimes again. People who have been in prison earn twenty five percent less money than before because having a criminal record makes it hard to find steady jobs. Starting their own business can give these people steady income when other employers will not hire them. People who have been in prison are more likely to start their own businesses than people who have never been in prison. Seven out of every one hundred people without criminal records start businesses, but twelve and a half out of every one hundred people with criminal records start businesses. Starting a business reduces the chance that formerly imprisoned people will commit crimes again by five and three tenths percent. This is a thirty two and a half percent drop from the normal rate of repeat crimes. Formerly imprisoned people who start their own businesses make two thousand seven hundred dollars more each year than formerly imprisoned people who work for others. The income difference between formerly imprisoned business owners and business owners without criminal records is thirty eight percent smaller than the income difference between formerly imprisoned employees and employees without criminal records.

sections.title: Section 3 Entrepreneurship Counseling and Training for Justice Impacted Individuals
sections.text: This section changes the Small Business Act by moving one section and adding a new section forty nine called the New Start Program. The new section defines important terms. Appropriate committees of Congress means the Committee on Small Business and Entrepreneurship of the Senate and the Committee on Small Business of the House of Representatives. Community Advantage Small Business Lending Company means companies that follow rules in federal regulations about lending money to small businesses. Currently incarcerated individual means a person who is locked up in a federal prison that is minimum, low, or medium security, or who lives in a halfway house run by the federal prison system, and who meets certain requirements about their crimes. Entrepreneurial development program means training that teaches people how to start or grow a small business, including self study courses, classroom teaching, one on one training, business planning help, mentorship programs, and small amounts of startup money. Formerly incarcerated individual means a person who finished serving time in federal, state, or local jail or prison and who meets certain requirements about their crimes. Microloan intermediary means organizations that can participate in a federal program that gives small loans to businesses. The Program means the New Start Program. SCORE means the Service Corps of Retired Executives. Women's business center means centers that help women start businesses. The Administrator must create the New Start Program within one hundred eighty days after this law is passed. The program will give grants to organizations for five years to create or support business training programs for people who are currently in prison or who used to be in prison. The Administrator must work with the Director of the Bureau of Prisons to let grant receiving organizations provide business help to people currently in prison. The Administrator must make a plan that gets updated every year to match organizations with formerly and currently imprisoned people. The Administrator should try to give equal amounts of grants to organizations that help formerly imprisoned people and organizations that help currently imprisoned people. The Administrator will give grants to organizations including small business development centers, women's business centers, and SCORE chapters. Each grant will be more than one hundred thousand dollars but less than five hundred thousand dollars each year for five years. The Administrator will make sure grant recipients are spread out across the United States. Women's business center grants under this program do not count toward other grant limits. Organizations can work together in partnerships when applying for grants and running programs. To apply for a grant, organizations must submit an application with information the Administrator requires. Applications must show that for programs helping formerly imprisoned people, the applicant is or partners with a lender that can provide money to qualified formerly imprisoned people. Applications must show strong community connections including with justice impacted communities, local businesses, and political leaders. Applications must show ability to provide full business development training on an ongoing basis. Applications must include a plan for reaching formerly and currently imprisoned people including specific target groups. Applications must clearly explain business development abilities including working with existing local partners for additional training. Applications must present a business development curriculum which can be a nationally recognized model. Applications must list all partner organizations. Applications must include a detailed plan for using grant money including estimates for administrative and outreach costs. The Administrator may give priority to applicants based on whether they have committed non federal funding, whether they consider local economies and markets in their education, whether they can provide business services along with job training, whether they have a history of effectively helping formerly or currently imprisoned people, and whether they will help currently imprisoned people who will be released within eighteen months. Grant recipients must connect formerly imprisoned people to federal resources including microloan programs, Community Advantage Small Business Lending Companies, small business development centers, women's business centers, SCORE chapters, Veteran Business Outreach Centers, and business centers run by the Minority Business Development Agency. The Administrator must submit yearly reports to Congress about the program including lists of grant recipients and partners, characteristics of people helped, participation rates, retention rates, reasons people do not complete programs, percentage of formerly imprisoned people who stay out of trouble, participant experiences, number and dollar amounts of loans made, and other required information. One year after the program ends, the Comptroller General must submit a report to Congress evaluating the services provided, oversight by the Administrator, and overall program performance and impacts. Nothing in this section affects existing microloan programs including their requirements, how they work, or available money. Congress authorizes the appropriation of whatever money is necessary to carry out this program.
